request.question: Who wrote the fantasy novella which was reworked in 2014 with a series of paintings by a Spanish artist born in Olalla in 1954?

title: La Leocadia
The Wall of Serpents
The Castle of Iron
Gaspar de la Huerta
The Roaring Trumpet
The Ice Dragon
The Green Magician
Luis Royo
The Mathematics of Magic
Pilgrimage to the Fountain of San Isidro
sentences: La Leocadia (Spanish: Doña Leocadia) or The Seductress (Spanish: Una Manola) are names given to a mural by the Spanish artist Francisco Goya (1746–1828), completed sometime between 1819–23, as one of his series of 14 "Black Paintings".
It shows a woman commonly identified as Goya's maid, companion and, most likely lover, Leocadia Weiss.
She is dressed in a dark, almost funeral maja dress, and leans against what is either a mantelpiece or burial mound, as she looks outward at the viewer with a sorrowful expression.

"Leocadia" is one of the final of the "Black Paintings", which he painted in his seventies at a time when he was consumed by political, physical and psychological turmoil, after he fled to the country from his position as court painter in Madrid.
The Wall of Serpents is a fantasy novella by American science fiction and fantasy authors L. Sprague de Camp and Fletcher Pratt.
The fourth story in their Harold Shea series, it was first published in the June 1953 issue of the fantasy pulp magazine "Fantasy Fiction".

It first appeared in book form, together with its sequel, "The Green Magician", in the collection "Wall of Serpents", issued in hardcover by Avalon Books in 1960; the book has been reissued by a number of other publishers since.
It has also been reprinted in various anthologies and collections, including "Great Short Novels of Adult Fantasy I" (1972), "The Complete Compleat Enchanter" (1989), and "" (2007).
It has been translated into Italian and German.
The Castle of Iron is the title of a fantasy novella by American authors L. Sprague de Camp and Fletcher Pratt, and of the novel into which it was later expanded by the same authors.
It was the third story (and afterwards the second volume) in their Harold Shea series.
As a 35,000 word novella it was first published in the fantasy magazine "Unknown" for April, 1941.
The revised and expanded novel version was first published in hardcover by Gnome Press in 1950, and in paperback by Pyramid Books in 1962.
The book has been reprinted by a number of other publishers since its first appearance.
An E-book edition was published by Gollancz's SF Gateway imprint on September 29, 2011 as part of a general release of de Camp's works in electronic form.
The novel has been combined with other books in the series in the omnibus editions "The Compleat Enchanter" (1975), "The Complete Compleat Enchanter" (1989) and "" (2007).
It has also been translated into Italian.
Gaspar de la Huerta (1645–1714) was a Spanish artist born at Campillo de Altobuey in Cuenca.

At an early age, seeking instruction in Valencia, he fell into the hands of Jesualda Sanchez, the bustling widow of Pedro Infant, a third-rate painter, who continued on her own account her husband's school for the manufacture of religious pictures.
La Huerta, nevertheless, attained some skill as a draughtsman and colourist, and married the widow's well-dowered daughter.
Working for moderate prices he found abundant employment in the neighbouring churches and convents.
For the Franciscans he painted the "Jubilee of the Porciuncula", and for the Dominicans the picture which long served as a veil to the wondrous image of "Our Lady of the Forsaken".
He died at Valencia in 1714.
The Museum of Valencia possesses a pleasing picture of "Christ and the Virgin enthroned", the latter with the bright complexion peculiar to Valencian beauty.
"The Roaring Trumpet" is a fantasy novella by American writers L. Sprague de Camp and Fletcher Pratt.
The initial story in their Harold Shea series, it was first published in the May 1940 issue of the fantasy pulp magazine "Unknown".

It first appeared in book form, together with its sequel, "The Mathematics of Magic", in the collection "The Incomplete Enchanter", issued in hardcover by Henry Holt and Company in 1941, and in paperback by Pyramid Books in 1960.

It has since been reprinted in various collections by numerous other publishers, including "The Compleat Enchanter" (1975), "The Incompleat Enchanter" (1979), "The Complete Compleat Enchanter" (1989), and "" (2007).
It has been translated into Dutch and Italian.
In 2016, the story was shortlisted for the Retro Hugo Award for Best Novella.
The Ice Dragon is a children's fantasy novella by George R. R. Martin, originally published in 1980 in the Ace Books anthology "Dragons of Light".
It was later included in Martin's 1987 collection "Portraits of His Children".
The story was reworked in 2007 with artwork by Yvonne Gilbert and again in 2014 with a series of original paintings by Luis Royo.
The Green Magician is a fantasy novella by American writers L. Sprague de Camp and Fletcher Pratt.
The fifth story in their Harold Shea series, it was first published in the November 1954 issue of the fantasy pulp magazine "Beyond Fiction".

It first appeared in book form, together with "The Wall of Serpents", in the collection "Wall of Serpents", issued in hardcover by Avalon Books in 1960; the book has been reissued by a number of other publishers since.

It has also been reprinted in various magazines, anthologies and collections, including "The Dragon" (June–July 1978), "The Complete Compleat Enchanter" (1989), "Masterpieces of Fantasy and Enchantment" (1988), and "" (2007).
It has been translated into Italian and German.
Luis Royo (born 1954) is a Spanish artist born in Olalla, a village in the Aragonese province of Teruel.

He is best known for his fantasy illustrations published in numerous art books, magazines such as "Heavy Metal" and various other media including book and music CD covers, video games and Tarot cards.
"The Mathematics of Magic" is a fantasy novella by American writers L. Sprague de Camp and Fletcher Pratt, the second story in their Harold Shea series.
It was first published in the August 1940 issue of the fantasy pulp magazine "Unknown".

It first appeared in book form, together with the preceding novella, "The Roaring Trumpet", in the collection "The Incomplete Enchanter", issued in hardcover by Henry Holt and Company in 1941, and in paperback by Pyramid Books in 1960.

It has since been reprinted in various collections by numerous other publishers, including "The Compleat Enchanter" (1975), "The Incompleat Enchanter" (1979), "The Complete Compleat Enchanter" (1989), and "" (2007).
It has been translated into Dutch and Italian.
In 2016, the story was shortlisted for the Retro Hugo Award for Best Novella.

Pilgrimage to the Fountain of San Isidro or The Holy Office (Spanish: Peregrinación a la fuente de San Isidro or El Santo Official ) are names given to an oil mural by the Spanish artist Francisco Goya (1746–1828), probably completed between 1821 and 1823.
The mural is one of the fourteen Black Paintings that Goya applied in oil on the plaster walls of his house.
Between 1874 and 1878 the paintings were transferred to canvas supports under the direction of the art restorer of the Museo del Prado, Salvador Martinez Cubells.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: Who wrote the fantasy novella which was reworked in 2014 with a series of paintings by a Spanish artist born in Olalla in 1954?
hop1_docs: The Ice Dragon | The Ice Dragon is a children's fantasy novella by George R. R. Martin, originally published in 1980 in the Ace Books anthology "Dragons of Light".  It was later included in Martin's 1987 collection "Portraits of His Children".  The story was reworked in 2007 with artwork by Yvonne Gilbert and again in 2014 with a series of original paintings by Luis Royo.

Luis Royo | Luis Royo (born 1954) is a Spanish artist born in Olalla, a village in the Aragonese province of Teruel.  He is best known for his fantasy illustrations published in numerous art books, magazines such as "Heavy Metal" and various other media including book and music CD covers, video games and Tarot cards.

Les Femmes d'Alger | Les Femmes d'Alger (Women of Algiers) is a series of 15 paintings and numerous drawings by the Spanish cubist artist Pablo Picasso.  The series, created in 1954-1955, was inspired by Eugène Delacroix's 1834 painting "The Women of Algiers in their Apartment" (French: "Femmes d'Alger dans leur appartement" ).  The series is one of several painted by Picasso in tribute to artists that he admired.
Jesús Olalla | Jesús "Josu" Olalla Iraeta (born July 15, 1971) is a Spanish handball player who competed in the 1996 Summer Olympics and in the 2000 Summer Olympics.

Chris Martin (artist) | Chris Martin (born 1954) is an American artist based in Brooklyn, New York.  Martin was born in Washington DC.  He makes abstract paintings with elements of collage which reference New Age and nature imagery.  Martin is influenced by artists such as Paul Feeley, Yayoi Kusama, Alfred Jensen and others who are also concerned with the formal qualities of painting.

Sarah Dollard | Sarah Dollard (born 21 January 1980) is an Australian screenwriter, living and working in the United Kingdom.  She made her start in writing for television on the long-running Australian soap opera "Neighbours", before moving to the UK in 2008.  Stints working in the script departments of British fantasy and science fiction TV shows, "Merlin" and "Primeval", led to writing episodes of BBC Three supernatural television series "Being Human" and British spy thriller "The Game", which first broadcast on BBC America.  She created and wrote the Welsh romantic comedy series "Cara Fi" (Love Me), which debuted on S4C in 2014.  She wrote the episode "Face the Raven" for Series 9 and the episode "Thin Ice" for Series 10 of Doctor Who.

The Copper Promise | The Copper Promise is a 2013 fantasy novella series by Jen Williams and her debut work.  It was initially released between December 2013 and January 2014 as a set of four e-book novellas and has since been published in paperback format as a single volume through Headline (ISBN  ).
response.summary_1: George R. R. Martin — author of the fantasy novella "The Ice Dragon" (reworked in 2014 with paintings by Luis Royo).

hop2_docs: The Ice Dragon | The Ice Dragon is a children's fantasy novella by George R. R. Martin, originally published in 1980 in the Ace Books anthology "Dragons of Light".  It was later included in Martin's 1987 collection "Portraits of His Children".  The story was reworked in 2007 with artwork by Yvonne Gilbert and again in 2014 with a series of original paintings by Luis Royo.

Luis Royo | Luis Royo (born 1954) is a Spanish artist born in Olalla, a village in the Aragonese province of Teruel.  He is best known for his fantasy illustrations published in numerous art books, magazines such as "Heavy Metal" and various other media including book and music CD covers, video games and Tarot cards.

The Sons of the Dragon | The Sons of the Dragon is a novelette by George R. R. Martin.  It is set in the Westeros of Martin's "A Song of Ice and Fire" series, hundreds of years before the events of "A Game of Thrones" (1996) during the reigns of two Targaryen kings, Aenys I and Maegor I "The Cruel", both sons of the first Targaryen king, Aegon I "The Conqueror".  Following an early leak of the novella's inclusion in the anthology "The Book of Swords" by Martin's longtime friend Gardner Dozois, Martin himself confirmed that the anthology was scheduled to be released on October 10, 2017.

Malefic Time | Malefic Time is a project created by Luis Royo and Romulo Royo.  It is set in a post apocalyptic world that mixes reality led to the decay of our society with real manifestations of mythological beliefs and superstitions of different cultures.

Rafael Royo-Torres | Dr. Rafael Royo-Torres is a Spanish Paleontologist.  He works in Fundación Conjunto Paleontológico de Teruel-Dinópolis, in Teruel, Spain.  He is one of the discoverers of "Turiasaurus riodevensis", together with Luis Alcalá and Alberto Cobos.

A Song of Ice and Fire | A Song of Ice and Fire is a series of epic fantasy novels by the American novelist and screenwriter George R. R. Martin.  He began the first volume of the series, "A Game of Thrones", in 1991 and had it published in 1996.  Martin, who initially envisioned the series as a trilogy, has published five out of a planned seven volumes.  The fifth and most recent volume of the series published in 2011, "A Dance with Dragons", took Martin five years to write.  He is still writing the sixth novel, "The Winds of Winter".

High Sparrow | The High Sparrow is a fictional character in the "A Song of Ice and Fire" series of high fantasy novels by American author George R. R. Martin and its television adaptation "Game of Thrones".  His real name is unknown.  The High Sparrow first appears in "A Feast for Crows" (2005).  He subsequently appeared in Martin's "A Dance with Dragons" (2011).
hop2_titles: A Song of Ice and Fire
